summary:
The Salt March, also known as the Dandi March or Salt Satyagraha, was a nonviolent civil disobedience campaign led by Mahatma Gandhi in 1930 to protest the British colonial government's monopoly on salt production and the salt tax. Beginning on 12 March 1930, Gandhi and 78 selected followers walked approximately 390 kilometers from his ashram at Sabarmati to the coastal village of Dandi, where on 5 April they produced salt from seawater in deliberate defiance of British law.

The march galvanized the Indian independence movement. As Gandhi walked, thousands joined the procession and the action attracted international media attention, bringing the injustice of British colonial rule to a worldwide audience. After reaching Dandi, Gandhi was joined across India by tens of thousands of protesters making salt illegally, and the campaign spread to include boycotts of British cloth and other goods. The British arrested over 60,000 people, including Gandhi himself in May 1930.

The Salt March was a masterstroke of nonviolent strategy. Salt was a perfect vehicle for mass protest — it was produced and consumed by all Indians regardless of caste or religion, the tax was universally felt as unjust, and the act of making salt was simple enough for anyone to replicate. The brutal British response to peaceful protesters — including the attack on the Dharasana Salt Works where protesters were beaten by police as reporters watched — created international outrage.

The campaign demonstrated the power of nonviolent mass action to generate political pressure and moral legitimacy, influencing civil rights movements worldwide including the American [[event:2679|Civil Rights Movement]]. The Salt March is widely considered one of the pivotal moments in the struggle for [[event:2670|Indian independence]], which was finally achieved in 1947.
